responses: I have lived in Douglas Wyoming all my life.  I was born in February 1967 in Cheynne while my dad was serving in the legislature.

I was raised on a ranch south of Douglas but moved to town 25 years ago.  I am a Douglas High School graduate and have a BS in Agriculture Economics from the University of Wyoming.  I have served as a County Commissioner for the past 19.5 years and that has provided me with a wealth of knowledge and experience.  I have advocated for Converse County and Wyoming at the state and national level, including the US Departments of Energy, Interior, Agriculture, BLM and EPA.  Multiple use, streamlined regulations and local control are a regular theme when I am speaking to those agencies. I am the stadium announcer for Douglas Bearcat Football and have been a High School Basketball Official for 23 years.  My wife Tione and I are small business owners and very active in our community.  We have 2 sons and 1 granddaughter.

We need to continue investing in economic development and diversity by appropriately funding and directing the Business council so they can be an effective partner with local entities as they seek out and grow local businesses.  If we fail to stay in the game as some have suggested, we lose, plain and simple.  Economic development is a process not an event.  Our core industries of Tourism, Energy and Agriculture employee and provide a large part of our revenue.  We must continue to research new prospects and build upon existing partnerships to expand those opportunities.  Rare earth elements and the resurgence of uranium have huge potential for Wyoming.  Finally, we need to invest more into our ageing infrastructure.  Much of infrastructure is at an age where if we fail to invest now, the cost will become unbearable later.  Roads, Bridges, water, sewer and the like are basic quality of life needs we must maintain and improve.

First of all, we need to make sure health care is available.  Rural hospitals are in desperate shape and lack of care is real.  The health care transformation proposal from the Dept of Health was trying to help ensure this in the long term.  Unfortunately, the proposal was rejected by the feds. However, some of the ideas in the proposal are worth looking at for state implementation.    Another factor is the limited number of companies offering health insurance in WY, which has appeared to lead to higher premiums.  The real fact is that the problems with health care are larger than Wyoming and real reform and vision is needed at the national level.  We must do what we can, but also pressure Congress to do better.

* There should not be any large-scale sale or trade of public lands. Public lands in Wyoming are a treasurer and we must protect them.  They provide recreation, wildlife habitat, support agriculture, mineral wealth and a host of other benefits.
Limited trades that block up public land with the goal of providing better access and more efficient management should be considered, but in a restricted manner and only with local input.

Targeted property tax relief has been very effective in Wyoming.  As a County Commissioner I advocated for and voted to implement the highly successful Converse County Property Tax refund program.  Over the last 3 years we have refunded over $620,000 to residential owners in Converse County.  That works well in a county with significant mineral production.  I support letting Wyoming citizens vote on the 50% reduction initiative, however if it passes, local services will be cut in many cities, towns and counties, the math is that simple.  Local governments will have to make very tough decisions, as I do not expect the legislature to be able to fill that gap as they will also need to fill the gap in school funding caused by the reduction.

*As noted before, we must remain in the economic development game.  If we fail to play, we lose.  Some have characterized economic development as picking winners and losers, that is incredibly short-sighted.  It is about creating jobs for our kids and grandkids.  It is about reducing our dependence on mineral production and increasing job diversity.   Activity breeds activity and sound economic development strategies will pay dividends.  One thing the state can do to help is investment into infrastructure to support work force housing.  In many places economic development is hampered by the limited places for new workers to live.  If we can help with the infrastructure, we can lower the cost of the residential lot and get affordable houses for our workforce.  We also need to be sure the state vision includes local input.  Each community will have different desires, and we need to accommodate local visions.

I believe the campaign reporting requirements for candidates is clear and transparent, which is very important.  Filing donation reports a week before the primary allows the voters to know who is supporting each candidate and evaluate accordingly.

questions: Where do you live currently? How long have you lived there? How long have you lived in Wyoming? Where were you born?
What age will you be on Election Day, Nov. 3, 2026?
Please tell the voters about yourself including your background and qualifications.
What are the biggest challenges and opportunities facing Wyoming today? As a legislator, how would you address them?

We asked WyoFile readers to rank issues that are important to them, and healthcare costs and access topped the list. What can the Legislature do to make healthcare more affordable and accessible to Wyomingites?
Do you support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming? How do you plan to ensure that state and federal land is managed in the best interest of the people of Wyoming?
No two Wyoming counties are the same when it comes to how they generate revenue. As a legislator, how would you approach the public's desire for property tax relief with local revenue needs?
What role, if any, should the Wyoming Legislature play in economic development?
Explain your values when it comes to campaign donations. Does Wyoming's system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures need reform?
pageProps.questionnaireIntro:
The answers below were solicited from candidates via a written questionnaire created by WyoFile reporters and editors in June 2026. Responses are presented exactly as submitted, without fact-checking, wordsmithing or editing for grammar, punctuation or spelling errors. 

Legislative candidates were invited to respond to the questionnaire several times by email and by phone. Out of fairness to the candidates who met the deadline, WyoFile will not add responses after the guide's publication.
pageProps.aboutProject:
WyoFile produced this guide with coding and web development by Thomas Musselman, editing by Tennessee Watson and production assistance by Calla Shosh. Contact Tennessee Watson with questions, corrections or suggestions at <tennessee@wyofile.com>.

WyoFile is a Wyoming 501(c)3 nonprofit, independent, member-supported, public-interest news service reporting on the people, places and policy of Wyoming. WyoFile's [donor base](https://wyofile.com/supporters/) is politically diverse, including some donors who are candidates in this year's election. We maintain separation between sources of revenue and editorial direction, decisions and oversight. Acceptance of financial support does not constitute implied or actual endorsement of donors or their products, services or opinions, and does not confer editorial influence, nor does donor support imply any endorsement of WyoFile's reporting, commentary or editorial position.

This material is available for republication by select media outlets. Contact Collaborations Editor Rebecca Huntington at <rebecca@wyofile.com> to learn how.
